# Transcrição: 07- 70 - Closer

- **Fonte:** 07- 70 - Closer
- **Duração:** 10:08
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Congratulations. We've reached the end of anatomy of a story. I know you haven't reached the end of your story. I know there's still work to do. There's still work to do in my story too. We actually have to apply sentences, words, punctuation, all of those things to actually have a story that we can speak out loud. But if you'll remember, seemingly a long time ago, probably, I told you that storytellers are first and foremost decision makers. The best decision makers make the best storytellers. I've taken you through the same process that I use, the same decision making process that I use before I begin actually speaking my story out loud. It's time for you to do that. Now, some people write their stories out on paper and that's great. And some people speak their stories out loud and that's also fantastic. Whatever method you want to use is great. My suggestion to you is this. If you're committing your story to paper, say it out loud first. Oftentimes, when we write, we do not write in the voice that we speak with. Those two things tend to be very different. On the page, we tend to be more formal. And when we speak out loud, we tend not to be. We speak in incomplete sentences. We speak in fragments. We speak in run on sentences. And that's what normal speech sounds like. So if you've decided to write your story down, which is totally fine and most storytellers write their stories down, speak it out loud first. Say the words so that they sound natural coming out of your mouth and then record them the way you speak them on the page. I'll also encourage you not to memorize your story. Instead, I'd like you to remember your story, which is to say you'll probably memorize the first couple lines of your story because you want to get off to a good start. You'll probably memorize tricky transition moments and lines that are especially funny. You'll remember the last couple sentences of your story because you want to land it well. But in the middle, I want you to remember your story instead of memorizing it. When we memorize stories, what happens is every word becomes a chain to another word. They're all links. And that's dangerous as a storyteller. When you build a chain of words, that means if you forget a single word, your chain breaks and you suddenly don't know what to say. So instead, let's remember our story. One of the best ways to remember your story is to remember the scenes of your story. Remember what your first scene is. Look at your paper. Look at your anatomy of a story worksheet. What is that first scene? What happens in it? What do I need to tell the audience over the course of this scene? What details are important? How do I transition to the second scene? Just remember what's in the scene and start to speak out loud. Figure out the sentences that

## [03:02 - 06:04]

make the most sense for you. When they sound good in your ears, write them down. When you're finished writing them down, read that story aloud to yourself. Record it while you're reading it. And then listen back. Does it sound like me? Does it sound natural? Did I miss anything? Does anything sound clunky? Are there more darlings to kill? Listening to our stories as we record them can be a really wonderful way to figure out if the story is working. Where are the boring parts? Where are the parts that need to be cut? What did I forget or what did I leave out? What still needs to be included that I've never even considered putting in? And then when you want to remember your story, one of the best ways to remember it is to just listen to it. Practicing a story is great and I practice my stories out loud all the time, but eventually you get bored telling your own story. It gets really annoying to tell your story again and again to yourself, but it doesn't get so boring to listen to it. From folding laundry, I'm listening to my story. I'm grocery shopping. I'm listening to my story. I'm driving my kids to school. I'm making them listen to my story. Listening to your story even passively will allow that story to sort of seep into your bones. It'll become a part of you before you know it, and it's easy to do. You can move through your life listening to a story about yourself told by you, which is just about the most narcissistic thing a person can do, but is also a highly effective way to remembering your story. So your job now is to look at all the decisions that we've made, each one of them step by step. In front of you now, you should have a worksheet, an anatomy of a story worksheet where all of your decisions are made, and you now just have to find the right sentences to apply to those decisions. Admittedly, it's not always easy. Finding the right way to say something can be really tricky. Here's one more strategy I'm going to give you that might help you along the way. You don't need to craft your story in order. Don't get hung up on any one particular moment. If you were to listen to early versions of many of my stories, you would hear me speaking for a while, and then you would hear me say something like this, insert excellent transition sentence here, and then I will continue talking, which is to say, I can't figure out how to transition from scene three to four. I need a sentence, but rather than sitting on that moment for a day, I'm just going to acknowledge that I have to find something eventually, but I'm not going to allow myself to stop. I'm not going to have writer's block while waiting for the right sentence to come to me. I'll often say things like, insert excellent anecdote that shows this here, but I have no idea what anecdote to choose, or I have yet to find an anecdote that actually works with the story.

## [06:05 - 09:06]

Again, it's my acknowledgement that something has to go here. I don't know what has to go here. I may never know what has to go here, but I'm going to move on for now. I'm going to finish my story. Oftentimes when I get to the end of my story, when I figured out sort of what I want to say, there were these holes in my story that need to be filled. And I go back and I start to try to fill them. Oftentimes when I get to the end, those holes are more easily filled because I can start telling myself the story and those transitions will suddenly pop into my head. Those anecdotes will suddenly occur to me once I have the totality of the story in front of me. So afford yourself that opportunity. Don't get stuck on any one moment. Just keep going. Get to the end. You know what the end is already. We always start with the end. It's on your worksheet. You know where you're going, so begin making those sentence by sentence decisions. When you get to the end, you'll have a story, and that is fantastic. There's a lot more to learn. I have lots to teach. And admittedly, what I've done is taken you through the very basic decision making process of telling a story. There's lots more to learn, but this, if you do this right, you're truly going to be better than most people. I would say almost everyone telling stories today because most storytellers don't make decisions before they open their mouth. Most people just start talking. And the first thing they say is the first thing that comes to their mind. And the second thing they say is the second thing that comes to their mind. They don't start with a plan. They don't make any decisions ahead of time. They don't have a path. They don't have a thesis statement. They don't know where to go. You do. You have the entire path, the entire plan, right in front of you. So it's time to get started. Go complete your story. Find the sentences that will bring that story to the greatest clarity possible, and then go tell someone that story. Choose someone who is a good person. Choose the kindest, gentlest, most wonderful person in your life. Choose the person who you know will say good things while also providing critical feedback. Don't choose the jerk in your life. Don't choose the awful human being who occupies spaces of your life. Don't tell them the first draft of your story. You deserve better than that. You deserve kindness. You deserve consideration. You deserve someone who will listen attentively, tell you what you've done well, and then perhaps give you some critical feedback to make it better. Eventually, you'll be happy with your story, and you'll take it to an audience. Maybe that audience is going to be three people at a dinner table or your kids on a Friday night. Maybe you're going to tell it to your colleagues around the conference table at the end of a meeting, or maybe you'll take it to a theater and tell thousands

## [09:06 - 10:08]

of people your story. All of those audiences are perfectly valid. I can't wait for your story to be done, and I can't wait to hear how it goes. Once you've done this story, start your next story. Storytellers are people who relentlessly find and craft stories from their everyday life. You have all the tools. Re-watch the videos if you need to. Go through the decision-making process again until it becomes second nature, until you don't need the worksheet anymore and you don't need the videos. Get to the point where all of these decisions become automatic for you. Continue to craft stories using this method until it becomes second nature. It will, I promise. I wish you the best of luck. I wish you a wonderful journey in storytelling. I wish you the greatest audience possible when your story is done and you're ready to tell it for the first time. Good luck.
